modules.focus: Navigating the workspace, panels, and tools to establish a comfortable editing environment.

Open and arrange multiple documents within the workspace
Customize and save a personalized workspace layout for efficient editing

Explain the difference between raster and vector graphics
Save files using appropriate formats such as PSD, JPEG, and PNG

modules.focus: Managing different elements of a composition using the Layers panel.

Reorder layers and use layer groups to organize design elements
Adjust layer opacity to control the visibility of specific elements

Utilize the Quick Selection and Magic Wand tools for color-based selections

Adjust canvas size independently of the image layer

Use Hue/Saturation to modify specific colors in an image
Understand how to use clipping masks to restrict adjustments to a single layer

modules.focus: Removing blemishes, dust, and unwanted objects from photographs.

Clone areas of an image using the Clone Stamp tool
Remove larger unwanted objects with the Content-Aware Fill feature

Draw and customize vector shapes using the Shape tools

Paint directly on layers using the Brush tool

Apply Smart Filters that can be adjusted or removed at any time

modules.title: Getting Started with the Photoshop Interface
modules.content:
Imagine opening a physical art studio for the first time. The walls are lined with brushes, palette knives, and canvases of varying sizes. There are mixing palettes, reference photos pinned to the walls, and a central easel waiting for you. Before you can paint a masterpiece, you need to know where the brushes are stored, how to turn on the overhead lighting, and where to place your canvas so you don't strain your back. 

Adobe Photoshop is your digital art studio. When you first launch the software, you are greeted by a dense array of menus, panels, and icons. It can feel overwhelming, but this complexity is an illusion. Every single item on your screen serves a specific, practical purpose. 

Before you can begin retouching photographs, designing graphics, or manipulating pixels, you must learn how to navigate your workspace. This chapter will guide you through the core components of the Photoshop interface, teaching you how to open documents, arrange your environment, and customize the layout so you can work efficiently and comfortably.

## The Anatomy of the Workspace

When you open Photoshop, the layout you see is called the **workspace**. The workspace is the entire digital environment where your editing takes place. While workspaces can be customized, Adobe provides a default layout that contains several distinct regions. 

Think of the workspace as a physical desk. You have your main desk area (where your active paper sits), your most frequently used tools kept within arm's reach, and reference materials stored in drawers or stacked on the sides. 

Here are the primary components of the default workspace:

### The Document Window

At the center of your screen is the **Document Window**. This is your digital canvas. When you open an image, it appears here. If you have multiple images open, they will appear as separate tabs at the top of the Document Window, much like tabs in a web browser. You can click these tabs to switch between your active documents. 

### The Tools Panel

Typically anchored to the far left of your screen is the **Tools panel**. This is your digital toolbox. It contains icons representing the various instruments you use to edit your images, such as brushes, erasers, text creators, and selection wands. 

Many of the tools in this panel are grouped with similar, related tools. You can identify a grouped tool by looking for a tiny triangle in the bottom-right corner of the tool's icon. 
*   To select a visible tool, simply left-click its icon.
*   To reveal the hidden tools in a group, **right-click** (or click and hold) the visible tool icon. A small fly-out menu will appear, allowing you to select the alternative tool.

### The Options Bar

Running horizontally across the top of the screen, just below the main menus, is the **Options bar**. This is a context-sensitive area. "Context-sensitive" means that the contents of this bar change depending on which tool you currently have selected in the Tools panel.

For example, if you select the Brush tool from the Tools panel, the Options bar will display settings for brush size, hardness, and opacity. If you then select the Text tool, the Options bar will change to show font families, font sizes, and text alignment options. Whenever you select a new tool, glance at the Options bar to see what specific settings you can adjust for that exact tool.

### The Application Frame and Menu Bar

Photoshop operates within an **Application Frame** on both Windows and macOS (though macOS allows you to disable this frame if you prefer floating windows). At the very top of this frame is the **Menu bar**. 

The Menu bar contains standard dropdown menus like *File*, *Edit*, *Image*, *Layer*, and *Filter*. While the Tools panel and Options bar give you quick access to frequent actions, the Menu bar houses the exhaustive list of every command Photoshop can execute. 

### Panels and the Panel Dock

On the right side of your screen, you will find the **Panel Dock**. Panels are small windows that contain controls, settings, and information about your project. The specific panels you see by default might include the Layers panel, the Color panel, and the Properties panel.

Panels are designed to keep your workspace uncluttered. They operate in two modes:
*   **Collapsed:** The panel is reduced to just an icon. This saves screen space. 
*   **Expanded:** The panel is open, displaying its full set of controls.

To expand a collapsed panel, simply click its icon once. To collapse it again, click the icon a second time. 

Some panels are grouped together into tabs. If you see a panel you want to view, click its named tab at the top of the panel window. If you want to pull a panel out of the dock to move it closer to your Document Window, click and drag the panel's tab away from the dock. To put it back, drag it by its tab and hover over the dock until a blue highlight appears, then release your mouse.

## Opening and Arranging Documents

A professional editor rarely works on a single image in isolation. You might be comparing a before-and-after state, pulling a graphic from one file into another, or batch-editing a series of photos. Knowing how to open and arrange multiple documents is essential for an efficient workflow.

### Opening Files

To open an existing image, navigate to the *File* menu at the top left of the Menu bar and select **Open**. This launches your operating system's file browser, allowing you to locate and select the image you wish to edit. You can also open files directly from your computer’s file explorer by clicking and dragging the image file directly onto the Photoshop workspace.

### Navigating Between Documents

When you have multiple documents open, Photoshop organizes them in the Document Window as a series of tabs. The active document—the one you are currently working on—will have a highlighted tab. To switch to another document, simply click its tab. 

If you prefer a more visual way to navigate, go to the *Window* menu at the top of the screen and select **Arrange**. Here, you can choose options like "Match All," which matches the zoom level and location of all open documents, allowing you to pan across multiple images simultaneously.

### Arranging Document Windows

Sometimes, viewing images in tabs is inefficient, especially if you need to compare two images side-by-side. Photoshop provides several built-in arrangements for this.

1. Go to the **Window** menu.
2. Hover over **Arrange**.
3. Select an arrangement option, such as **2-up Vertical** or **2-up Horizontal**.

This will take your active documents and tile them neatly on your screen. If you want to manually arrange them, simply click and drag the document tab away from the top bar. The document will become a floating window that you can move independently. To place it back into the tabbed layout, click and drag the floating window's tab back up to the top tab bar until you see a blue highlight, then release.

## Customizing and Saving Your Workspace

Every editor has a unique workflow. A photographer retouching portraits will rely heavily on different tools and panels than a graphic designer creating a web layout. Photoshop allows you to tailor the workspace to your specific needs, ensuring the tools you use most are always visible, while the ones you don't are tucked away.

### A Realistic Scenario: The Portrait Retoucher

Imagine you are retouching a series of portrait photographs. Your primary focus is on color correction, skin smoothing, and detailed brushwork. 

In the default workspace, you might find panels like "3D" or "Measurements" taking up space in the Panel Dock—panels you have no use for. Meanwhile, you constantly need access to the "History" panel (which allows you to undo multiple steps) and the "Brush Settings" panel. 

Instead of hunting through the *Window* menu every time you need these tools, you can build a custom workspace.

### Building Your Custom Layout

To customize your workspace, you use the **Window** menu. This menu contains a list of every panel available in Photoshop. 

1. **Add Panels:** Go to *Window* and click on the name of a panel you want to add (e.g., *Window > History*). The panel will appear on your screen, likely as a floating window.
2. **Dock Panels:** Click and drag the new panel by its tab into the Panel Dock on the right. Drop it where you want it to live. You can place it above, below, or next to existing panels.
3. **Remove Panels:** To remove a panel you don't need, right-click its tab and select **Close**. Alternatively, you can click and drag the panel's tab out of the dock to make it a floating window, and then click the "X" in the corner of that window.
4. **Adjust the Tools Panel:** You can even collapse the Tools panel into a single column of icons to save horizontal screen space by clicking the double arrows at the very top of the panel.

### Saving the Workspace

Once you have arranged the panels, tools, and document windows exactly to your liking, you need to save this layout. If you don't save it, Photoshop will revert to the default layout the next time you restart the software.

1. Go to the **Window** menu.
2. Select **Workspace > New Workspace**.
3. A dialog box will appear. Type a name for your new workspace (e.g., "Portrait Retouching").
4. Ensure the checkboxes for "Keyboard Shortcuts" and "Menus" are checked if you want to save any customizations you made to those elements.
5. Click **Save**.

### Switching Between Workspaces

Once saved, your custom workspace is always available. Look at the top-right corner of the Photoshop interface, just below the Menu bar. You will see a row of workspace names (like "Essentials," "Photography," and "Graphics and Web"). Your newly saved workspace will appear here as a clickable button. 

You can switch back and forth between different workspaces instantly by clicking these names. If you ever make changes to your saved workspace and want to restore it to the way you originally saved it, simply go to *Window > Workspace > Reset [Your Workspace Name]*.

## Key Takeaways

*   The **Workspace** is the entire Photoshop environment, composed of the Document Window, Tools panel, Options bar, Menu bar, and Panel Dock.
*   The **Tools panel** (left side) holds your editing instruments. Look for a small triangle in the corner of an icon to find hidden, related tools.
*   The **Options bar** (top) is context-sensitive; its settings change depending on which tool you currently have selected.
*   **Panels** (right side) contain project settings and controls. They can be collapsed into icons to save space or expanded for full access.
*   You can open multiple documents at once and view them as tabs or arrange them side-by-side using the **Window > Arrange** menu.
*   You can add, remove, and reposition panels to create a layout that fits your specific editing style, and save this layout via **Window > Workspace > New Workspace**.

modules.title: Document Creation and File Basics
modules.content:
Imagine designing a stunning promotional poster for a local business. You spend hours perfecting the layout, adding vibrant colors, and applying intricate details. You proudly send the file to a professional print shop, eagerly awaiting the final product. When you pick up the printed poster, however, the image looks blurry and pixelated, and the vibrant blues you carefully selected have printed as a dull, muddy purple. 

What went wrong? The culprit is almost certainly a mismatch in document settings—specifically, resolution and color mode. Before you can ever create a masterpiece in Photoshop, you have to build the right foundation. 

In the previous chapter, you explored the Photoshop workspace, learned how to arrange your **Document Window**, and customized your panels. Now, it is time to actually create something inside that workspace. This chapter will guide you through the essential first steps of any Photoshop project: creating a new document with the correct physical dimensions, resolution, and color mode. You will also learn the fundamental difference between the two types of graphics Photoshop handles (raster and vector), and how to properly save your work using the right file formats.

## Raster vs. Vector Graphics: The Building Blocks

Before we create a new file, you need to understand what kind of art you are making. Photoshop is primarily known as a **raster image editor**, but it also has the ability to work with **vector graphics**. Understanding the difference between these two is crucial for choosing the right settings later on.

### Raster Graphics (Pixels)
A **raster graphic** (sometimes called a bitmap image) is made up of a grid of tiny individual squares called **pixels**. If you open a photograph in Photoshop and zoom in far enough, you will eventually see that the image breaks down into a mosaic of colored squares. 

*   **Best used for:** Photographs, digital paintings, and highly detailed images with complex gradients and textures.
*   **The catch:** Raster graphics are **resolution-dependent**. This means they have a fixed number of pixels. If you create a small raster image and try to scale it up to be much larger, Photoshop has to stretch the existing pixels. Because there aren't enough pixels to fill the new, larger space, the image becomes blurry, blocky, and pixelated.

### Vector Graphics (Math)
**Vector graphics**, on the other hand, are not made of pixels. They are created using mathematical formulas to define shapes, lines, and curves. When you draw a vector circle, Photoshop isn't lighting up a specific grid of squares; it is calculating the geometry of that circle based on a mathematical equation.

*   **Best used for:** Logos, typography, icons, and illustrations that require crisp, hard edges.
*   **The advantage:** Vector graphics are **resolution-independent**. Because they are drawn with math, you can scale a vector logo from the size of a postage stamp to the size of a billboard without losing a single drop of quality. The math simply recalculates, and the edges remain razor-sharp.

Photoshop is unique because it handles both. While your photographs will always be rasterized pixels, the text and shape tools (which we will cover in later chapters) generate vector paths. When you create a new document, you are primarily setting up the grid for your raster pixels, which is why resolution is so important.

## Creating a New Document

To start a new project, you can use the **Menu bar** at the top of the **Application Frame** and go to **File > New**, or you can use the keyboard shortcut Ctrl+N (Windows) or Cmd+N (Mac). 

This opens the New Document dialog box. It can look a bit overwhelming at first, but it is essentially just a recipe for your blank canvas. The dialog box is divided into a few main areas:

1.  **Top Tabs:** You can start from a blank canvas (Print, Photo, Web, Mobile, Film & Video) or open a recently used file. Clicking one of these categories gives you preset sizes tailored to that medium.
2.  **Preset Details (Right Panel):** This is where the actual numbers live. No matter what preset you click on the left, you can manually adjust the specifics on the right.
3.  **Presets and Search:** A quick way to find specific standard sizes, like "US Letter" or "Instagram Post."

### Setting Dimensions

**Dimensions** dictate the physical size of your canvas. You can set the Width and Height using various units of measurement. The unit you choose depends on your final output:

*   **Inches / Centimeters:** Use these for anything that will be physically printed (posters, flyers, business cards).
*   **Pixels:** Use this for anything that will live on a screen (websites, social media graphics, app designs). Screens are measured in pixels, so it makes sense to design for them in pixels.

*Scenario:* If you are hired to design a standard business card, you would set your Width to 3.5 Inches and your Height to 2 Inches. If you are making an Instagram post, you might set your Width and Height to 1080 by 1080 Pixels.

### Understanding Resolution

**Resolution** determines how densely packed the pixels are within your set dimensions. It is measured in **PPI** (Pixels Per Inch) for screens, though you will often hear the older term **DPI** (Dots Per Inch) used interchangeably, which technically refers to printer ink dots. 

Resolution only matters when an image has physical dimensions (like inches). If your document is measured purely in pixels (like a 1080 x 1080 pixel web graphic), the resolution setting is mathematically irrelevant because there are no inches involved.

But for print, resolution is the difference between a crisp poster and a blurry mess. 

*   **For Print:** The standard resolution is **300 PPI**. This means there are 300 pixels squeezed into every inch of the printed paper. From a normal viewing distance, the human eye cannot distinguish the individual pixels, making the image look smooth and continuous.
*   **For Web/Screen:** The standard resolution is **72 PPI**. Modern screens are highly variable in their pixel density, so web images are generally evaluated by their total pixel dimensions (e.g., 1920 x 1080) rather than PPI. However, setting a web document to 72 PPI is a long-standing convention.

If you set a print document to 72 PPI, Photoshop will spread the pixels too thinly across the paper, resulting in a pixelated print. If you set a web document to 300 PPI, the image will have far more pixels than a screen needs to display it, resulting in an unnecessarily large file size that slows down your website without improving visual quality.

### Color Mode

The **Color Mode** determines the mathematical model Photoshop uses to describe and reproduce colors in your document. For beginners, there are two you need to care about right now:

*   **RGB (Red, Green, Blue):** This is an **additive** color model. It works by combining red, green, and blue light together. When all three colors of light are combined at full intensity, they create white. RGB is the language of light, which makes it the perfect color mode for anything viewed on a screen—monitors, phones, and televisions all emit light in RGB.
*   **CMYK (Cyan, Magenta, Yellow, Key/Black):** This is a **subtractive** color model. It works by applying physical ink to paper. The inks absorb (subtract) certain wavelengths of light and reflect others. When you mix Cyan, Magenta, and Yellow ink together, they create a dark, muddy brownish-black (which is why pure Black ink—Key—is added to the mix). CMYK is the standard color mode for commercial printing.

*Scenario:* If you design a vibrant blue logo in RGB mode and send it to a print shop without converting it to CMYK, the printer will try to replicate that bright blue light-color using dark, physical inks. The result will be a dull, unexpected color. Always start your document in the color mode that matches your final output medium.

### Background Contents

This final setting simply dictates what your canvas looks like when the document opens. You can choose:
*   **White:** Fills the canvas with solid white.
*   **Black:** Fills the canvas with solid black.
*   **Background Color:** Fills the canvas with whatever color is currently set as your background color in the **Tools panel**.
*   **Transparent:** Creates a canvas with no background at all, represented by a gray and white checkerboard pattern. This is highly useful when you are creating elements (like a logo) that you want to place over other images later without a white box surrounding them.

Once you have set your dimensions, resolution, color mode, and background contents, click the **Create** button. Your new blank document will appear in the **Document Window**, ready for you to work on.

## Saving Your Work: File Formats Explained

Once you have created your document and added some content (even if it's just a few brush strokes or a single shape), you must save your work. Saving frequently is the golden rule of digital design.

To save a file for the first time, go to the **Menu bar** and select **File > Save** (or **File > Save As** if you want to create a copy). This opens the Save dialog box where you name your file and choose where it lives on your computer. 

The most critical choice here is the **Format** dropdown menu. Different file formats serve entirely different purposes. Choosing the wrong one can flatten your work, destroy your quality, or make the file unusable for its intended platform.

### PSD (Photoshop Document)

The **.psd** format is Photoshop's native file format. This is your "working file." 

*   **Pros:** It preserves everything you do in Photoshop. It saves your layers, your masks, your text as editable text, your shapes as editable vectors, and even your guides and panel positions. As long as you are still working on a project, you should be saving it as a PSD.
*   **Cons:** PSDs are large files. Furthermore, they are proprietary to Adobe. If you send a PSD to someone who doesn't have Photoshop (or a program that can read PSDs), they won't be able to open it. 
*   **When to use:** Always. Save a PSD version of your work as your master backup.

### JPEG (Joint Photographic Experts Group)

The **.jpg** or **.jpeg** format is the most common format for photographs on the web. 

*   **Pros:** It supports millions of colors and uses **lossy compression**. "Lossy" means that to achieve a smaller file size, the format permanently throws away visual data that the human eye is less likely to notice. You can usually choose a quality setting (from 1 to 12 in Photoshop) to balance file size and visual quality.
*   **Cons:** Because it uses lossy compression, every time you open a JPEG, make a change, and save it again, the image loses more quality. This is called "generation loss." Additionally, JPEGs do not support transparency. If you have a transparent background and save as a JPEG, Photoshop will automatically fill that transparent area with solid white. JPEGs also flatten all layers; you cannot save a multi-layer document as a JPEG and expect to edit those layers later.
*   **When to use:** For final, finished photographs and continuous-tone images destined for the web or email.

### PNG (Portable Network Graphics)

The **.png** format was created as a modern, superior alternative to the older GIF format, specifically for web use.

*   **Pros:** It uses **lossless compression**, meaning it reduces file size without throwing away any visual data. A PNG can be opened, edited, and saved repeatedly without any loss in quality. Most importantly for designers, PNG supports full **transparency**. You can save an object with a transparent background, and it will remain transparent when placed on a website.
*   **Cons:** PNG files are generally larger than JPEGs, especially for complex photographs. Because they preserve every detail perfectly, they aren't as efficient for vast, photographic images on the web where tiny details aren't as critical. Like JPEGs, PNGs flatten your layers upon saving.
*   **When to use:** For web graphics that require transparency (like logos or icons), or for images with sharp edges, text, or large areas of solid color where JPEG compression would create visible artifacts (blurry, blocky patches).

### A Note on Other Formats
You will see many other formats in the Save dialog box, such as GIF, TIFF, and PDF. 
*   **TIFF** is excellent for high-quality print images, but results in massive file sizes. 
*   **PDF** is great for sharing documents that need to be viewed universally, and it can preserve Photoshop editing capabilities. 
*   **GIF** is an older format largely used for simple web animations today.

For now, as a beginner, focusing on PSD for working, JPEG for web photos, and PNG for web graphics with transparency will cover 99% of your needs. (We will cover advanced exporting for specific web and print workflows in Chapter 11).

## The Save Workflow

When you finish a design session, your workflow should look something like this:

1.  Go to **File > Save** (or **Save As**). 
2.  Choose a location on your computer (ideally a dedicated project folder).
3.  Name your file logically (e.g., `AcmeCorp_Logo_Draft_v1`).
4.  Select **Photoshop (*.PSD, *.PDD)** from the Format dropdown.
5.  Click **Save**. 

This secures your master file. If you need to send a preview to a client, you would then go to **File > Save As** again, change the name to `AcmeCorp_Logo_Preview`, and change the Format to PNG or JPEG. You now have two files: your editable master, and a flattened, shareable copy.

## Key Takeaways

*   **Raster graphics** are made of pixels and are resolution-dependent (scaling them up causes blurriness). **Vector graphics** use math and are resolution-independent (they scale infinitely without losing quality).
*   When creating a new document, always set your **dimensions** based on the final output (inches for print, pixels for web).
*   **Resolution** dictates pixel density. Use **300 PPI** for print and **72 PPI** for web/screen.
*   **Color Mode** dictates how colors are mixed. Use **RGB** for anything viewed on a screen, and **CMYK** for anything physically printed with ink.
*   Always save your working files as **PSD** to preserve your layers and editable elements.
*   Use **JPEG** for final web photographs (no transparency, lossy compression) and **PNG** for web graphics requiring transparency or sharp edges (lossless compression).

modules.title: Understanding Layers and Layer Organization
modules.content:
## The Magic of Transparent Acetate

Imagine you are designing a movie poster. You have a background photograph of a cityscape, a cut-out image of the hero, a glowing logo, and some text at the bottom. If you were working with physical materials, painting all of these elements onto a single canvas would be a nightmare. If the hero's arm accidentally overlapped the logo, you would have to paint over the logo, redraw the arm, and hope you didn't ruin the background in the process.

Digital design solves this problem using a concept called **layers**. 

Think of layers as individual, transparent sheets of acetate stacked on top of one another. You can draw, paste, or paint on one sheet without ever touching the others. If you don't like how the hero's arm looks, you simply remove that specific sheet of acetate, replace it with a new one, and drop it back into the stack. The background and the logo remain perfectly intact.

When you look at the stack from above, you see a single, flattened composition. But beneath the surface, every element is isolated and editable. By the end of this chapter, you will know exactly how to create, manage, and organize these layers using the **Layers panel**—the command center for your document's depth.

## Touring the Layers Panel

In Chapter 1, we explored the Photoshop workspace and the Panel Dock. The **Layers panel** is arguably the most important panel in the entire application. If it isn't currently visible on your screen, go to the Menu bar, select **Window**, and click on **Layers**. 

When you open the Layers panel, you are looking at a vertical list of every layer in your document. Here are the essential parts of this panel you need to know before we start editing:

*   **Layer Name:** By default, Photoshop names layers generically (like "Layer 1" or "Layer 2"). As we will see, renaming them is a crucial organizational step.
*   **Eye Icon (Visibility Toggle):** Clicking this icon hides the layer from the Document Window, making it invisible. Click the empty space where the eye was to make it visible again.
*   **Active Layer (Highlight):** Whatever layer is currently highlighted in blue (or gray, depending on your operating system) is the **active layer**. If you pick up a brush or use a tool, it will only affect this specific layer.
*   **Layer Thumbnail:** A small preview image of what is actually on that transparent sheet of acetate. The checkerboard pattern behind the image indicates transparency.
*   **Opacity Slider:** Located at the top right of the panel, this controls how see-through the active layer is. We will explore this in depth later in this chapter.
*   **Bottom Toolbar:** A row of icons at the very bottom of the panel used for adding effects, grouping, adding masks (which we will cover in Chapter 4), creating adjustment layers (Chapter 6), and deleting layers.

## Creating, Duplicating, and Deleting Layers

Every new Photoshop document starts with a single layer. If you created a document using the basics from Chapter 2, you likely saw a layer named "Background" sitting in the Layers panel. To build a composition, you need to know how to add to it, copy it, and remove from it.

### Creating New Layers
Creating a new blank layer gives you a fresh, transparent sheet of acetate to work on. 

To create a new layer, click the **Create a New Layer** icon at the bottom of the Layers panel (it looks like a square with a folded corner and a plus sign). The new layer will appear directly above whatever layer was previously active. 

Alternatively, you can use the keyboard shortcut: **Shift + Ctrl + N** (Windows) or **Shift + Cmd + N** (Mac). This will pop up a dialog box allowing you to type a name for the layer before it is created.

### Duplicating Layers
Sometimes you don't want a blank layer; you want an exact copy of an existing one. Maybe you want to duplicate a shape to create a shadow, or you want to edit a copy of an image while keeping the original safe.

To duplicate a layer, click and drag the layer's name down onto the **Create a New Layer** icon at the bottom of the panel. A carbon copy of that layer will appear above the original, with the word "copy" appended to its name.

You can also right-click directly on the layer's name in the Layers panel and select **Duplicate Layer** from the contextual menu.

### Deleting Layers
If you make a mistake or no longer need an element, you can delete the layer entirely. 

To delete a layer, click and drag the layer's name down to the **Trash Can** icon at the very bottom right of the Layers panel. You can also select the layer and simply click the Trash Can icon. 

**A word of caution:** Deleting a layer removes everything on that transparent sheet. If you are used to pressing the Delete or Backspace key on your keyboard, be aware that this only clears the *pixels* on the active layer, leaving you with an empty, transparent layer rather than removing the layer from the panel entirely.

## The Vertical Hierarchy: Reordering Layers

The order in which layers are stacked in the Layers panel directly dictates how your composition looks in the Document Window. 

Photoshop reads layers from **bottom to top**. The layer at the very bottom of the panel is at the back of your canvas. The layer at the very top of the panel is at the very front of your canvas. 

If you have a layer with a photograph of a dog, and a layer above it filled entirely with the color blue, you will not see the dog. The blue layer is in front of the dog layer, completely covering it. 

To change this, you simply reorder the layers:
1. Click and hold your mouse button on the layer you want to move.
2. Drag it upward or downward in the Layers panel. As you drag, you will see a thin, highlighted line appear between other layers, indicating where the layer will drop.
3. Release the mouse button when the highlight line is in your desired position.

### A Realistic Scenario: The Coffee Shop Flyer
Imagine you are designing a flyer for a local coffee shop. You have three layers: a background color, a photograph of a coffee cup, and text that says "Grand Opening."

Currently, your stack looks like this from top to bottom:
1. Background Color
2. Coffee Cup Photo
3. "Grand Opening" Text

If you look at your Document Window, you will only see the background color. The coffee cup and the text are trapped behind it. 

To fix this, you click and drag the "Coffee Cup Photo" layer above the "Background Color" layer. Suddenly, the cup appears on your canvas. Next, you click and drag the "Grand Opening" Text layer to the very top of the stack. Now, the text sits proudly in front of the coffee cup. 

## Staying Organized with Layer Groups

As your designs become more complex, your layer count will grow. A simple poster might have twenty or thirty layers. A complex web design might have hundreds. If you leave them all as a flat list in the Layers panel, finding the specific layer you need to edit becomes a frustrating game of hide-and-seek.

Photoshop solves this with **Layer Groups**. A Layer Group is essentially a folder that lives inside your Layers panel. You can place related layers inside this folder to collapse them out of sight, keeping your panel clean and manageable.

### Creating and Naming Groups
To create a Layer Group, click the **Create a New Group** icon at the bottom of the Layers panel (it looks like a small folder). An empty folder will appear at the top of your layer stack. 

Just like layers, groups default to the name "Group 1." To rename it, double-click the group's name slowly, type something descriptive like "Header Text" or "Background Elements," and press Enter.

### Populating Groups
To put layers into a group, simply click and drag the layer names on top of the group folder icon. When the folder icon highlights, release the mouse. The layer will indent slightly, indicating it is now inside the folder.

You can put as many layers as you want into a single group, and you can even nest groups inside of other groups for highly complex organizational structures. 

To view the layers inside a group, click the small triangle or chevron icon to the left of the folder icon. This expands or collapses the group. Collapsing groups you aren't currently working on is the single best way to maintain your sanity when navigating large Photoshop files.

### Moving Groups
When you reorder a group in the Layers panel, every layer inside that group moves with it. This allows you to keep entire sections of your design—like all the text for a poster, or all the elements of a logo—together while adjusting their overall position in the vertical hierarchy.

## Controlling Visibility with Layer Opacity

Until now, we have treated layers as all-or-nothing: a layer is either 100% visible, or it is hidden entirely via the Eye icon. However, one of the most powerful features of layers is the ability to make them semi-transparent.

The **Opacity** slider, located in the top right of the Layers panel, controls the transparency of the active layer. 

*   **100% Opacity:** The layer is completely solid. It completely obscures whatever is behind it.
*   **0% Opacity:** The layer is completely invisible. 
*   **50% Opacity:** The layer is half-transparent. You can see the layer's contents, but you can also clearly see the layers behind it bleeding through.

To adjust the opacity, simply select a layer, click the small arrow next to the percentage, and drag the slider left or right. A faster method is to move your mouse over the word "Opacity," click and hold, and drag left or right to scrub the value. If you have the Move tool selected, you can simply type a number on your keyboard (like "5" for 50% or "8" for 80%) to instantly change the active layer's opacity.

### Practical Applications for Opacity
Opacity is not just a gimmick; it is a foundational design tool. Here are a few ways you will use it:

*   **Creating Ghosted Backgrounds:** If you have a text layer that is hard to read over a busy background photograph, you might duplicate the background, darken it, and drop its opacity to 40%. This creates a subtle, faded backdrop that allows the text to pop without completely losing the context of the photo.
*   **Layering Textures:** If you place a layer of grungy paper texture over a clean digital design, it will look fake and overwhelming at 100%. Dropping the texture layer to 20% or 30% opacity allows the texture to blend naturally into the design beneath it.
*   **Building Shadows:** You can duplicate a shape, fill the copy with black, and drop its opacity to 15% to create a realistic, soft shadow effect beneath the original shape.

## Essential Layer Habits for Beginners

Before we move on to selections and masking in the next chapter, it is vital to establish a few professional habits. The difference between a beginner and a proficient Photoshop user is rarely about knowing fancy tools; it is almost always about organization.

1.  **Name Your Layers Immediately:** "Layer 4" tells you nothing. "Blue Circle" or "Hero Photo" tells you exactly what is on that layer. Get into the habit of double-clicking a layer's name and renaming it the moment you create it.
2.  **Group Early and Often:** Don't wait until you have thirty layers to start grouping. Once you have two or three layers that belong to the same logical part of your design, create a group and put them in it.
3.  **Check Your Active Layer:** Before you paint, type, or edit, glance at the Layers panel. Is the layer you want to edit highlighted? A massive percentage of beginner mistakes happen because the user was editing the wrong active layer.
4.  **Use the Eye Icon to Isolate:** If you are trying to edit a specific element but the canvas is visually cluttered, toggle the Eye icon off on the surrounding layers. Isolate what you are working on, make your edits, and turn the rest back on.

## Key Takeaways

*   **Layers** are transparent sheets that stack vertically, allowing you to edit individual elements of a composition without affecting others.
*   The **Layers panel** is the control center. The layer highlighted in blue is the **active layer**, meaning it is the only layer that will be affected by your tools.
*   You can **create**, **duplicate**, and **delete** layers using the icons at the bottom of the Layers panel.
*   Photoshop reads layers from **bottom to top**. The layer at the top of the panel appears at the front of the Document Window. Clicking and dragging layer names allows you to reorder them.
*   **Layer Groups** (folders) keep your panel organized by bundling related layers together. Collapsing groups reduces visual clutter.
*   The **Opacity** slider controls a layer's transparency, allowing underlying layers to show through. This is essential for creating textures, shadows, and visual depth.

modules.title: Basic Selections and Layer Masks
modules.content:
Imagine you have a photograph of a beautiful mountain landscape, but the sky is disappointingly gray and washed out. You want to replace it with a vibrant blue sky from another image. If you simply paste the new sky over the old one, it will cover the entire photo—including your mountains. 

To fix this, you need a way to tell Photoshop exactly which part of the image you want to change (the sky) and which parts you want to leave alone (the mountains). This process of isolating a specific area is called making a **selection**. 

Selections are the foundation of all targeted editing in Photoshop. They allow you to paint, color correct, or remove backgrounds with surgical precision. But making a selection is only half the process. Once you have isolated an area, you need a way to apply that change without permanently destroying your original image. This is where **layer masks** come in. Together, selections and layer masks form the core of a non-destructive Photoshop workflow.

## The Mechanics of Selecting

When you make a selection in Photoshop, you are essentially placing a temporary boundary—visible as a flashing dashed line often called "marching ants"—around a specific area of your image. 

When a selection is active, anything you do—whether painting with a brush, filling with color, or applying an adjustment—will only affect the area inside those marching ants. The unselected area is completely protected. 

Before we dive into the specific tools, you need to know how to manage a selection once you have made it. 

### The Four Selection States

As you work with selections, you will constantly need to adjust them. Here are the essential keyboard shortcuts that control selection states:

*   **Deselect:** To remove a selection entirely, go to **Select > Deselect** in the Menu bar, or press `Ctrl+D` (Windows) / `Cmd+D` (Mac).
*   **Select All:** To select the entire contents of your active layer, press `Ctrl+A` (Windows) / `Cmd+A` (Mac).
*   **Inverse:** Sometimes it is easier to select the thing you *don't* want, and then flip the selection. For example, if you select a person standing against a solid background, you can easily select the background and then invert it. Go to **Select > Inverse**, or press `Ctrl+Shift+I` (Windows) / `Cmd+Shift+I` (Mac).

### Adding To and Subtracting From Selections

Rarely will you create a perfect selection on your first try. Most selection tools allow you to modify an existing selection using the modifiers in the **Options bar** (the horizontal bar beneath the Menu bar). When a selection tool is active, look at the far left of the Options bar to see four icons:

1.  **New Selection:** This is the default. Making a new selection will discard any existing selection.
2.  **Add to Selection:** Allows you to draw another selection. If it overlaps your first selection, they will combine into one larger selection. You can also trigger this temporarily by holding down the `Shift` key while dragging.
3.  **Subtract from Selection:** Allows you to carve away parts of your current selection. You can trigger this temporarily by holding down the `Alt` (Windows) / `Option` (Mac) key while dragging.
4.  **Intersect with Selection:** Keeps only the overlapping area where your new selection crosses your existing selection. 

## Geometric Selections: The Marquee Tools

The simplest way to make a selection is by using the **Marquee tools**. Located at the very top of the Tools panel, the Marquee toolset contains the Rectangular Marquee and the Elliptical Marquee. 

### The Rectangular Marquee Tool

The **Rectangular Marquee Tool** does exactly what it sounds like: it draws rectangular or square selections. 

1.  Click and hold on the Marquee tool icon in the Tools panel to reveal the fly-out menu, and select the Rectangular Marquee.
2.  Click and drag diagonally across your image to draw a box. 
3.  Release the mouse button. The marching ants will appear, defining your rectangular selection.

**Pro Tip: Perfecting the Shape**
If you want a perfect square, hold down the `Shift` key *while* you drag. If you want to draw your selection outward from the center point where you clicked (rather than from the corner), hold down the `Alt` (Windows) / `Option` (Mac) key while dragging. You can combine both—holding `Shift` and `Alt`/`Option` together—to draw a perfect square from the center.

### The Elliptical Marquee Tool

The **Elliptical Marquee Tool** works identically to the Rectangular Marquee, but it draws oval or circular selections. This is incredibly useful for selecting round objects like clocks, wheels, or the moon. 

You select it by clicking and holding the Marquee icon in the Tools panel and choosing the Elliptical Marquee from the fly-out menu. Just like the rectangular version, holding `Shift` constrains it to a perfect circle, and holding `Alt`/`Option` draws it from the center outward.

### Eliminating Edge Fringing

When making selections—especially with the Elliptical Marquee—you might notice that a thin halo of the original background color remains around the edge of your selected object. This is called **fringing**. 

To fix this, go to the Menu bar and choose **Select > Modify > Contract**. Enter a value of 1 or 2 pixels. This pulls the marching ants inward by that amount, cutting off the background halo. Alternatively, you can use **Select > Modify > Feather**, which softens the edges of the selection rather than cutting them abruptly, allowing for smoother blending.

## Color-Based Selections: Quick Selection and Magic Wand

Geometric selections are great for straight lines and perfect circles, but the real world is full of irregular shapes. When you need to select a subject with complex, uneven edges, Photoshop offers tools that look for differences in color and contrast to do the heavy lifting for you.

### The Quick Selection Tool

The **Quick Selection Tool** (located just below the Marquee tools in the Tools panel) acts like an intelligent paintbrush. As you drag over an area, Photoshop analyzes the colors and textures under your brush and automatically expands the selection to include similar adjacent pixels.

1.  Select the Quick Selection Tool from the Tools panel.
2.  Look at the Options bar. You can adjust the **brush size** using the bracket keys on your keyboard: `[` makes the brush smaller, and `]` makes it brush larger. A smaller brush gives you more precision for tight edges.
3.  Click and drag over the object you want to select. You will see the marching ants expand to cover the object.
4.  If the tool selects too much (grabbing the background along with your subject), switch to the **Subtract from Selection** mode in the Options bar (or hold `Alt`/`Option`) and paint over the areas you want to remove from the selection.

The Quick Selection tool is highly intuitive and often the fastest way to isolate a subject from a relatively contrasting background.

### The Magic Wand Tool

Hidden beneath the Quick Selection Tool in the Tools panel is the **Magic Wand Tool**. While the Quick Selection tool looks at broad areas of color and texture, the Magic Wand is designed to select contiguous areas of a single, specific color. This is ideal for selecting solid-color backgrounds, like a blue sky or a white studio backdrop.

When you select the Magic Wand, pay close attention to the Options bar, specifically the **Tolerance** setting. 

**Tolerance** dictates how strict the tool is about matching colors. 
*   A **low Tolerance** (e.g., 10) means the Magic Wand will only select pixels that are almost exactly the same color as the pixel you clicked. 
*   A **high Tolerance** (e.g., 60) means the tool will accept a wider range of similar colors. 

There is also a checkbox in the Options bar called **Contiguous**. 
*   If **Contiguous** is checked, the Magic Wand will only select matching pixels that are physically touching the pixel you clicked. 
*   If it is unchecked, it will select every pixel in the entire image that matches your Tolerance range, even if they are separated by other colors. 

**Scenario: Replacing a Sky**
Imagine you have a photo of a city skyline against a gray sky. You want to darken the sky without affecting the buildings. You select the Magic Wand, set the Tolerance to 20, ensure Contiguous is checked, and click on the gray sky. Because the sky is a relatively uniform color, the marching ants will perfectly wrap around the tops of the buildings, isolating the sky. If a few patches of sky between buildings are left unselected, hold `Shift` and click those patches to add them to your selection.

## Non-Destructive Editing: Introduction to Layer Masks

Making a selection is a temporary action. If you press `Ctrl+D` / `Cmd+D` to deselect, your boundary vanishes. Furthermore, if you make a selection and press the `Delete` key, you permanently erase those pixels from your layer. If you make a mistake and save the file, those pixels are gone forever.

To avoid destructive editing, Photoshop uses **Layer Masks**. 

A layer mask is an invisible grayscale overlay attached to a layer. It acts as a map that tells Photoshop which parts of the layer to show and which parts to hide. 

*   **White** on a mask means "show these pixels."
*   **Black** on a mask means "hide these pixels."
*   **Gray** means "partially show these pixels" (resulting in transparency or soft edges).

The golden rule of Photoshop is: **Black conceals, White reveals.**

Because a mask only hides pixels, the original image data remains completely intact beneath the mask. If you make a mistake, you can simply paint over the mask with white to bring the hidden pixels back.

### Creating a Layer Mask from a Selection

The most common way to use a layer mask is to convert an active selection into a mask. 

Let’s look at a practical scenario. You have two layers in your Document Window: a bottom layer showing a mountain range, and a top layer showing a perfectly blue sky. You want to hide the sky on the top layer so the mountains from the bottom layer show through.

1.  Make sure the top layer (the blue sky) is the active layer in your Layers panel.
2.  Use the Rectangular Marquee tool to select the bottom half of the image—where the mountains should be.
3.  Look at the bottom of the Layers panel. You will see an icon that looks like a rectangle with a circle cut out of the middle. This is the **Add Layer Mask** button. 
4.  Click that button.

Photoshop will instantly hide the selected portion of the top layer. The mountains from the bottom layer are now visible. If you look closely at the Layers panel, you will see that a new thumbnail—a black and white rectangle—has been added to the right of your top layer's image thumbnail. This is the **Layer Mask thumbnail**. The top half is white (the sky is showing), and the bottom half is black (the sky is hidden).

### Editing a Layer Mask

The true power of a layer mask lies in its editability. Once a mask is created, you can refine it using the Brush tool (which we will cover in depth in a later chapter, but the basic mechanics are useful to know now).

1.  **Target the Mask:** In the Layers panel, click directly on the Layer Mask thumbnail. You will see four small white brackets appear around the corners of the thumbnail. This indicates that the mask—not the image itself—is currently active for editing.
2.  Select the **Brush Tool** from the Tools panel.
3.  Set your Foreground color to Black. (You can do this by pressing `D` to reset your colors to default black and white, and `X` to swap them).
4.  Paint over an area of your image that is currently visible. As you paint with black, those pixels will disappear, revealing the layer beneath.
5.  Press `X` to swap your Foreground color to White. Paint over the area you just erased. The pixels will reappear.

This back-and-forth—painting with black to hide and white to reveal—is the essence of non-destructive editing. You can adjust your edges infinitely without ever damaging the original photograph.

### A Word of Caution: Mask vs. Image Linking

When you click the Add Layer Mask button, Photoshop creates a link between the image layer and the mask. You will see a small chain-link icon between the two thumbnails in the Layers panel. 

When they are linked, if you move the layer, the mask moves with it. If you click on that chain-link icon, it will disappear, unlinking the two. This allows you to move the mask independently of the image, or vice versa. For beginners, it is usually best to leave them linked.

## Key Takeaways

*   **Selections** are temporary boundaries (marching ants) that restrict edits to a specific area of an image.
*   You can modify selections using the icons in the Options bar, or by using keyboard shortcuts: `Shift` to add to a selection, and `Alt`/`Option` to subtract from one.
*   The **Rectangular** and **Elliptical Marquee tools** are best for geometric shapes. Holding `Shift` constres them to perfect squares or circles.
*   The **Quick Selection Tool** intelligently finds edges based on color and texture as you drag over an object.
*   The **Magic Wand Tool** selects pixels based on color similarity, controlled by the **Tolerance** setting in the Options bar.
*   **Layer Masks** are grayscale overlays attached to a layer. They hide or reveal pixels non-destructively.
*   Remember the golden rule of masks: **Black conceals, White reveals.** 
*   You can convert any active selection into a layer mask by clicking the **Add Layer Mask** button at the bottom of the Layers panel.
*   Always ensure the Layer Mask thumbnail is selected (surrounded by brackets) in the Layers panel before attempting to edit the mask with the Brush tool.

modules.title: Cropping and Transforming Images
modules.content:
## Reframing Reality: The Crop Tool

Imagine you’ve just taken a perfect portrait, but a stranger photobombed the edge of the frame. Or perhaps you shot a beautiful landscape, but the horizon line tilts at a distracting 15-degree angle. In traditional film photography, these issues might mean throwing the photo in the trash. In Photoshop, they are minor inconveniences easily fixed with the **Crop Tool**.

At its core, cropping is the act of removing unwanted outer areas from an image to improve its composition, focus the viewer's attention, or change the shape of the frame. 

To get started, select the **Crop Tool** from the Tools panel (it looks like two overlapping right angles, or you can press **C** on your keyboard). Once selected, a crop boundary will immediately appear around the edges of your image in the Document Window. 

### The Anatomy of the Crop Boundary

When the crop boundary appears, you will notice several interactive elements:
*   **Corner Handles:** Located at the four corners of the boundary. Dragging these allows you to scale the crop diagonally.
*   **Edge Handles:** Located in the center of the four sides. Dragging these adjusts the crop from one side at a time.
*   **Shield (Overlay):** The area outside your crop boundary will darken. This visual shield shows you exactly what will be deleted when you apply the crop, helping you focus on what will remain.

To adjust the crop, simply click and drag any of the handles inward. You can also click anywhere inside the boundary and drag to reposition the image behind the crop frame. 

### Applying or Canceling the Crop

Once you have adjusted the boundary to your liking, you need to apply the crop to make the changes permanent. You can do this by pressing **Enter** (Return on Mac) on your keyboard, or by clicking the checkmark icon in the Options bar at the top of the screen. If you change your mind, press **Esc** on your keyboard or click the crossed-out circle (Cancel) in the Options bar.

### Straightening a Tilted Image

A crooked horizon line is one of the most distracting elements in a photograph. The Crop Tool includes a built-in **Straighten Tool** to fix this with mathematical precision.

Let’s look at a realistic scenario: You took a photo of a beach sunset, but the ocean slopes downward to the right. 

1. Select the **Crop Tool**.
2. In the Options bar, click on the **Straighten Tool** icon (it looks like a small spirit level). 
3. Find a line in your image that *should* be perfectly horizontal—like the horizon where the ocean meets the sky. 
4. Click and drag a line across that horizon, matching its exact tilt.
5. Release the mouse. 

Photoshop will automatically rotate the image to make your drawn line perfectly horizontal. It will also automatically shrink the crop boundary to fit within the rotated image, ensuring you don’t end up with empty, transparent triangles in the corners of your final image. Press **Enter** to apply the straightened crop.

## Understanding Aspect Ratios

When you drag the corner handles of the Crop Tool, you are changing the **aspect ratio**—the proportional relationship between the width and height of an image. 

If you drag freely, the aspect ratio changes constantly. However, you often need your final image to fit a specific format. For example, an Instagram feed post requires a 1:1 (square) aspect ratio, while a standard high-definition screen requires a 16:9 (widescreen) aspect ratio.

In the Options bar, directly to the right of the Straighten Tool, you will find the **Aspect Ratio** dropdown menu. 

*   **Original Ratio:** Locks the crop boundary to the current proportions of your image.
*   **Preset Ratios:** Provides quick access to common sizes like 1:1 (Square), 4:5 (Portrait), and 16:9 (Widescreen).
*   **Width x Height x Resolution:** Allows you to type in exact measurements (e.g., 8 inches by 10 inches at 300 pixels per inch). 

If you select a preset like 1:1, the crop boundary will snap into a perfect square. No matter how you drag the handles, it will remain a square. To swap the orientation (e.g., changing a horizontal 16:9 to a vertical 9:16), simply click the double-pointing arrow icon between the width and height boxes in the Options bar.

### Deleting vs. Hiding Cropped Pixels

By default, when you apply a crop, Photoshop permanently deletes the pixels outside the boundary. However, if you look at the Options bar, you will see a checkbox labeled **Delete Cropped Pixels**. 

If you **uncheck** this box before applying your crop, Photoshop will not throw away the hidden pixels. Instead, it will resize your canvas to hide them, while keeping the full original image intact on the layer. If you select the Crop Tool again later, the hidden pixels will magically reappear, allowing you to readjust your crop. This is a highly recommended, non-destructive way to work, as it gives you the flexibility to change your mind later.

## Free Transform: Scaling, Rotating, and Skewing

While the Crop Tool alters the overall document frame, you will often need to alter the actual content sitting on a specific layer. Maybe you dragged a logo onto your canvas and it’s too big, or you need to rotate a picture frame to make it look like it's hanging on a wall. 

To manipulate a layer's content, we use the **Free Transform** command. 

To activate Free Transform, select your desired layer in the Layers panel, then press **Ctrl+T** (Windows) or **Cmd+T** (Mac). Alternatively, you can go to the Menu bar and select **Edit > Free Transform**. 

When activated, a bounding box with eight small square handles will appear around the edges of your layer. The cursor will change depending on where you hover over this box.

### Scaling (Resizing)

Scaling changes the physical size of the layer. 

*   **To scale freely:** Click and drag any of the corner handles. 
*   **To scale proportionally:** In recent versions of Photoshop, dragging a corner handle scales proportionally by default. If you need to distort the proportions (making an image wider without making it taller), hold down the **Shift** key while dragging a corner handle. *(Note: If you are using a very old version of Photoshop, this behavior is reversed—Shift locks the proportions).*
*   **To scale from the center:** Hold down the **Alt** (Windows) or **Option** (Mac) key while dragging a handle. The layer will expand or contract equally in all directions from its center point.

### Rotating

To rotate the layer, move your cursor just outside the bounding box—near, but not directly on, one of the corner handles. Your cursor will change into a curved, double-sided arrow. Click and drag to spin the layer around its center. 

If you need a precise rotation, look at the Options bar. You will see a **Angle** field (labeled with a degree symbol). You can type a specific number here (e.g., 45 or -90) to rotate the layer exactly that many degrees.

### Skewing and Distorting

Sometimes you need to push an image out of its rectangular shape entirely. When Free Transform is active, you can access these advanced transformations by **right-clicking** inside the bounding box. A context menu will appear.

*   **Skew:** Slants the layer along one axis. If you select Skew and drag a side handle, the top and bottom edges will remain parallel, but the side edges will slant. This is excellent for creating the illusion of perspective, like making a sign look like it’s receding into the distance.
*   **Distort:** Removes all constraints. You can grab any corner handle and drag it independently in any direction. This is useful for wrapping a text layer onto the surface of a folded piece of cloth or fitting a screen onto a blank monitor in a photo.
*   **Perspective:** When you drag a corner handle in Perspective mode, the opposite corner moves in the exact opposite direction. This creates a vanishing point effect, perfect for making a flat image look like it’s lying flat on a floor or road stretching toward the horizon.

### The Dangers of Scaling Up

When you scale a layer down, Photoshop simply throws away extra pixels, and the image remains sharp. However, when you scale a layer up, Photoshop has to invent new pixels to fill the larger space. This process is called **interpolation**. 

If you scale a small image up significantly, it will become blurry, soft, and pixelated. As a general rule, scaling down is safe, but scaling up should be done cautiously and in small increments. 

### Committing to a Transformation

Just like with the Crop Tool, a Free Transform is not permanent until you commit to it. While the bounding box is active, the rest of Photoshop is locked. You cannot use other tools or select other layers. 

To apply the transformation, press **Enter** (Return on Mac) or click the checkmark in the Options bar. To cancel the transformation and return the layer to its original state, press **Esc** or click the cancel icon.

## Adjusting the Canvas Size

It is crucial to understand the difference between an image layer and the document itself. Think of the document as a piece of paper on a desk, and the layer as a photograph glued to that paper. The Crop Tool cuts both the paper and the photograph simultaneously. Free Transform only resizes or rotates the photograph, leaving the paper alone. 

But what if you want to make the piece of paper larger without changing the size of the photograph? You need to adjust the **Canvas Size**.

Changing the canvas size alters the working area of your document. It does not scale, rotate, or alter the pixels of any layers inside it. It simply gives you more (or less) empty space to work with.

### Making the Canvas Larger

To adjust the canvas, go to the Menu bar and select **Image > Canvas Size**. A dialog box will appear displaying the current width and height of your document. 

Let’s use a scenario: You designed a poster, but your client just asked for a two-inch blank border at the bottom to add text later. 

1. Open the **Canvas Size** dialog box.
2. Ensure the **Relative** checkbox is checked. This tells Photoshop you want to *add* to the current size, rather than typing in a brand new total size.
3. Since you only want to add height to the bottom, type **2** in the Height field (set to inches), and **0** in the Width field.
4. Look at the **Anchor** grid below the dimensions. This 3x3 grid of arrows tells Photoshop where to add the new space. By default, the center square is clicked, meaning the canvas will expand equally in all directions. Click the **top-center arrow** instead. This anchors your current image to the top, forcing all the new two inches of space to be added to the bottom.
5. At the very bottom of the dialog box is the **Canvas Extension Color** dropdown. Since you are adding new space, you need to tell Photoshop what color to fill it with. Choose **White** (or any color you prefer).
6. Click **OK**.

Your document is now taller, but the poster layer itself remains exactly the same size, sitting at the top of the new, larger canvas. 

### Making the Canvas Smaller

You can also use the Canvas Size dialog box to make the document smaller. If you type in dimensions smaller than your current document and click OK, Photoshop will throw up a warning: "The new canvas size is smaller than the current canvas size; some clipping will occur." 

If you proceed, Photoshop will cut away the edges of the canvas. Any layer content that extends beyond the new boundaries will be hidden, though not permanently deleted from the layers themselves. While you can do this to crop your image, using the Crop Tool is generally much more visual and intuitive. Canvas Size is best used when you need exact, numerical additions or subtractions to your working area.

## Key Takeaways

*   The **Crop Tool** (shortcut **C**) alters the overall dimensions of your document, allowing you to reframe the composition and remove unwanted edges.
*   The **Straighten Tool**, found in the Crop Tool's Options bar, allows you to draw a line over a crooked horizon, and Photoshop will automatically rotate the image to make it level.
*   **Aspect Ratio** defines the proportional relationship between width and height. You can lock the crop to specific ratios (like 1:1 for square) using the Options bar dropdown.
*   Unchecking **Delete Cropped Pixels** before applying a crop is a non-destructive way to work, allowing you to recover hidden image areas later.
*   **Free Transform** (shortcut **Ctrl+T** / **Cmd+T**) allows you to scale, rotate, skew, distort, and apply perspective to the content of an individual layer without affecting the document size.
*   Scaling layers up significantly can cause pixelation and blurriness because Photoshop has to artificially invent new pixels.
*   **Canvas Size** (found under the Image menu) changes the size of the working document independently of the layers inside it, allowing you to add or remove blank working space.

modules.title: Tone and Color Correction with Adjustments
modules.content:
## The Non-Destructive Advantage

You took a photo, but the lighting was off. The image looks too dark, or maybe the colors look slightly unnatural. In the past, fixing this meant permanently altering the original pixels. If you made the image brighter and saved the file, the original shadow details were gone forever. 

In Photoshop, this permanent alteration is called a **destructive edit**. To avoid this, Photoshop offers **adjustment layers**. An adjustment layer is a special type of layer that alters the color or tone of the layers beneath it without actually changing their original pixels. 

Think of an adjustment layer as a pair of tinted sunglasses placed over your image. If you look through red sunglasses, the world looks red. If you take the sunglasses off, the world returns to normal. Adjustment layers work the same way: they sit above your image layers in the Layers panel, mathematically changing how the pixels appear on your screen. If you decide you don't like the effect, you can simply delete the adjustment layer, and your original image remains untouched. This approach is called **non-destructive editing**.

### Finding the Adjustments Panel

To create an adjustment layer, look for the **Adjustments panel**. By default, this panel is docked in the Panel Dock on the right side of your workspace. 

If you don't see it, go to the Menu bar at the top of the screen, click **Window**, and select **Adjustments** from the dropdown list. 

The Adjustments panel displays a grid of small icons, each representing a different type of color or tonal correction. When you hover your cursor over an icon, a tooltip will appear telling you its name. When you click an icon, Photoshop does two things simultaneously:
1. It creates a new adjustment layer in your Layers panel.
2. It opens the **Properties panel**, where you will find the specific sliders and controls for that adjustment.

## Correcting Exposure: Brightness/Contrast and Levels

**Exposure** refers to the amount of light captured in a photograph. An **overexposed** image is too bright, with washed-out highlights. An **underexposed** image is too dark, hiding details in the shadows. Photoshop gives us several tools to fix these lighting issues.

### The Quick Fix: Brightness/Contrast

The most straightforward adjustment tool is **Brightness/Contrast**. 

*   **Brightness** lightens or darkens the entire image evenly.
*   **Contrast** increases or decreases the difference between the light and dark areas of an image. High contrast makes lights lighter and darks darker; low contrast makes everything look flat and gray.

To use it, click the Brightness/Contrast icon in the Adjustments panel. In the Properties panel, you will see two sliders. Simply drag the Brightness slider to the right to lighten an underexposed photo, or drag the Contrast slider to the right to give a flat photo more punch. 

While Brightness/Contrast is incredibly easy to use, it is also the bluntest instrument. It treats every pixel equally. If you have a photo of a person standing in front of a bright window, increasing the Brightness slider will lighten the person’s face, but it will also make the already-bright window completely blown out. For more precise control, we use Levels.

### The Professional Fix: Levels

The **Levels** adjustment is one of the most powerful tools in Photoshop. Instead of applying a uniform change to the whole image, Levels allows you to target specific tonal ranges: the darkest shadows, the brightest highlights, and the middle tones (called **midtones**).

When you open a Levels adjustment layer, the Properties panel displays a graph called a **histogram**. A histogram is a visual map of the light and dark pixels in your image. The bottom of the graph represents a scale from pure black (0) on the left to pure white (255) on the right. The taller the graph is at any given point, the more pixels exist at that specific brightness level.

Beneath the histogram are three sliders:
*   **Black Point Slider (Left):** Dragging this to the right tells Photoshop, "Make the darkest parts of my image pure black."
*   **White Point Slider (Right):** Dragging this to the left tells Photoshop, "Make the brightest parts of my image pure white."
*   **Gray Point Slider (Middle):** Dragging this left or right adjusts the overall midtones, shifting the overall exposure lighter or darker without altering the absolute black or white points.

**A Realistic Scenario:** 
Imagine you took a landscape photo on a hazy day. The image looks flat and gray. You open a Levels adjustment layer and look at the histogram. You notice the graph doesn't reach the left or right edges; it is bunched up in the middle. This means your image lacks true blacks and true whites.

To fix this, you drag the Black Point slider to the right until it meets the left edge of the graph's data. Then, you drag the White Point slider to the left until it meets the right edge of the graph's data. Instantly, the haze clears. The shadows become deep and rich, the highlights become bright and crisp, and the overall contrast is restored. Finally, you nudge the Gray Point slider slightly to the left to brighten the midtones. The exposure is fixed, and the original pixels remain untouched.

## Modifying Color: Hue/Saturation

Once your exposure is balanced, you may need to correct or change the colors in your image. The go-to tool for this is the **Hue/Saturation** adjustment.

When you open a Hue/Saturation adjustment layer, the Properties panel presents three main sliders:
*   **Hue:** Hue is the actual name of the color (e.g., red, blue, green). Sliding the Hue dial shifts all the colors in your image around the color wheel. It’s the tool you use if you want to turn a red car into a blue car.
*   **Saturation:** Saturation is the intensity, or purity, of a color. Dragging the Saturation slider to the right makes colors more vivid and vibrant. Dragging it to the left makes them muted. Dragging it all the way to the left (-100) removes all color, turning the image black and white.
*   **Lightness:** This slider simply makes the colors lighter or darker.

By default, the Hue/Saturation panel is set to adjust the **Master**. This means if you move the Hue slider, every color in the image shifts simultaneously. 

### Targeting Specific Colors

Often, you only want to change one specific color. For example, a person’s skin might look too orange, or the sky might look slightly purple instead of blue. 

At the top of the Hue/Saturation Properties panel is a dropdown menu that defaults to "Master". If you click this menu, you can choose specific color families: Reds, Yellows, Greens, Cyans, Blues, or Magentas. 

If you select "Blues" and lower the Saturation slider, only the blue pixels in your image will become less saturated. The grass, the skin tones, and the buildings will remain completely unaffected. 

**Targeting with the On-Image Tool:**
Sometimes it is hard to know exactly which color family a specific pixel belongs to. Is that jacket yellow, red, or magenta? Instead of guessing from the dropdown menu, Photoshop offers a shortcut. 

In the Properties panel, directly to the left of the Master dropdown, there is a small icon of a hand pointing at a slider. This is the **On-Image adjustment tool**. 
1. Click that icon.
2. Move your cursor out onto your image in the Document Window. Your cursor will temporarily change into an eyedropper.
3. Click and hold on the exact color you want to change in your image.
4. Drag your cursor to the left to decrease saturation, or to the right to increase saturation. Photoshop will automatically identify the color family you clicked on, select it in the dropdown menu, and adjust the sliders for you.

## Restricting Adjustments with Clipping Masks

When you create an adjustment layer, it floats above all other layers in the Layers panel. By default, it affects every single layer beneath it. 

If you have a document with three layers—a background photo, a second photo on a layer above it, and an adjustment layer on top—adjusting the Hue/Saturation will change the colors of *both* photos. 

This is often exactly what you want. But what if you only want to change the color of the top photo? What if you need to darken the second photo without affecting the background?

To restrict an adjustment layer’s effect to only the single layer directly beneath it, we use a **clipping mask**. A clipping mask "clips" the adjustment layer to the layer below it, preventing the effect from spilling over onto the rest of the document.

### How to Create a Clipping Mask

There are two primary ways to apply a clipping mask to an adjustment layer.

**Method 1: The Properties Panel Shortcut**
1. Create your adjustment layer. Notice it affects all layers beneath it.
2. Look at the bottom of the Adjustments Properties panel. You will see an icon that looks like a square with a downward-pointing arrow attached to its bottom-left edge. 
3. Click this icon. 
4. In the Layers panel, the adjustment layer will shift slightly to the right, and a small downward arrow will appear on its thumbnail. This is the visual indicator that a clipping mask is active. The adjustment will now only affect the layer immediately below it.

**Method 2: The Layers Panel Shortcut**
1. Create your adjustment layer.
2. In the Layers panel, hover your cursor directly on the horizontal line separating the adjustment layer from the layer you want it to affect. 
3. Hold down the **Alt** key on Windows, or the **Option** key on a Mac. 
4. Your cursor will change into a small square with a downward-pointing arrow. 
5. Click the line. The clipping mask is created.
6. To remove the clipping mask, simply Alt/Option+click the line between the two layers again.

**A Realistic Scenario:**
Imagine you are designing a poster. You have a flat gray background on Layer 1, and a photo of a car on Layer 2. You want to turn the car into a vibrant, stylized illustration, so you add a Hue/Saturation adjustment layer, crank the saturation up, and shift the hue. Suddenly, your flat gray background also turns a bizarre neon color. 

To fix this, you simply apply a clipping mask to the Hue/Saturation layer. The adjustment is clipped to Layer 2. The car gets your vibrant color treatment, and the background instantly returns to its original flat gray. 

## Key Takeaways

*   **Adjustment layers** are non-destructive, meaning they alter the appearance of pixels without permanently changing the original image data.
*   **Brightness/Contrast** is a simple, blunt tool for quick fixes, while **Levels** offers precise control over shadows, midtones, and highlights using a histogram.
*   The **Hue/Saturation** adjustment modifies the actual color (Hue), its intensity (Saturation), and its brightness (Lightness).
*   By default, an adjustment layer affects all layers beneath it. Using a **clipping mask** restricts the adjustment so it only affects the single layer immediately below it in the Layers panel.
*   The **On-Image adjustment tool** in the Hue/Saturation panel allows you to click directly on a color in your image to target and adjust it automatically.

modules.title: Retouching and Healing Techniques
modules.content:
## The Art of Invisible Repairs

You have just taken the perfect portrait. The lighting is exactly right, the subject’s expression is natural, and the composition is flawless. But when you open the photograph on your computer, you spot them: a stray piece of lint on the subject’s dark sweater, a sudden breakout of acne on their chin, and a distracting piece of trash on the ground in the background. 

In the days of traditional darkroom photography, fixing these issues required painstaking manual labor with physical brushes and dyes. Today, Adobe Photoshop provides a dedicated set of tools designed specifically for these invisible repairs. This process is known as **retouching**—the act of altering an image to remove imperfections, distractions, or unwanted elements. 

Photoshop groups its repair tools under the category of **healing**. Healing tools do more than just copy one part of an image over another; they mathematically blend the texture, lighting, and shading of surrounding pixels to make the repair completely seamless. In this chapter, we will focus on three distinct techniques: using the Spot Healing Brush for quick fixes, the Clone Stamp Tool for precise manual copying, and Content-Aware Fill for removing larger, complex objects.

## Non-Destructive Retouching Setup

Before we touch any tools, it is vital to establish a safe workflow. In previous chapters, you learned about the power of layers and layer masks. When retouching, there is always a risk of accidentally painting over the wrong area or destroying valuable pixel information. 

To avoid permanent mistakes, we will use a non-destructive workflow. Instead of painting directly on the original image layer (often called the **Background layer**), we will create a new, blank layer above it to hold our retouching work. 

**To set up a non-destructive retouching layer:**
1. Open your image in the **Document Window**.
2. Go to the **Panel Dock** where your Layers panel is located.
3. Click the **Create a New Layer** icon (it looks like a square with a folded corner) at the bottom of the Layers panel.
